nativeRange.habitat: The Long-Band Rasbora inhabits shaded blackwater forest streams as well as slow-moving or still waters with abundant leaf litter, overhanging vegetation, and a gentle current. The water in these environments is tea-coloured from tannins and humic acids leaching out of decaying plant matter, and has very low mineral content.

decorPreferences: dark substrate (fine sand or small gravel) to enhance colour
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannin release and cover
dense planting with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Cryptocoryne, Rotala)
floating plants to diffuse lighting and mimic forest canopy shade

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, insects, worms, and crustaceans. In the aquarium, accepts quality micro-pellets and fine flake as staples; supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods such as brine shrimp nauplii, daphnia, cyclops, and finely chopped bloodworms. Feed 1–2 small portions daily; avoid overfeeding in soft, blackwater setups where water quality can decline quickly.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetra, neon tetra)
dwarf gouramis and peaceful labyrinth fish
avoidWith: larger or boisterous fish that may outcompete or intimidate

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by -serwacy01- (5184x3456). Tack-sharp planted-tank shot of a whole live fish showing the crisp, unbroken black mid-lateral band from snout to caudal peduncle; best diagnostic and quality pick.
Photo by Ahsan_al_hidayat (2592x1944). Sharp live specimen showing vibrant orange-gold body and green-iridescent black lateral stripe; out-of-water on white background but strong colour/ID.
Photo by Lerdsuwa (1300x866). Different individual in a planted aquarium showing the full black band; kept for context variety, slightly soft focus.

summary: The Long-Band Rasbora (Rasbora einthovenii) is a slender, mid-sized schooling fish from blackwater forest streams across the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. It is best known for the bold, unbroken black stripe running from snout to tail, which intensifies in soft, tannin-rich water.
careNotes: The Long-Band Rasbora is a blackwater specialist native to the shaded forest streams of the Malay Peninsula (including Singapore), Sumatra, Borneo, and southern Thailand. It is best kept in soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 1–10) with a temperature of 22–26°C. Its most distinctive feature is a continuous, bold black mid-lateral band stretching from the snout to the caudal peduncle — more defined and unbroken than in many similar rasboras, and intensified when the fish is kept in tannin-stained water. Recreating blackwater conditions using driftwood, dried Indian almond leaves, or peat filtration is strongly recommended, as colour, confidence, and long-term health are all notably improved in these conditions. A dark substrate and floating or densely planted surface cover to subdue lighting mirrors the species’ shaded forest-stream habitat. Keep in groups of at least 8 — larger schools of 12 or more produce more natural shoaling behaviour and reduce individual shyness. The Long-Band Rasbora is an active mid-water swimmer and an accomplished jumper; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Gentle filtration, such as a sponge filter or low-flow hang-on, is preferred, as strong current is uncharacteristic of the natural habitat. The intermediate care rating reflects the specific soft-water chemistry requirements; in hard or alkaline tap water, conditioning with RO/DI water or blackwater additives is necessary. The species is generally considered hardy once settled into appropriate water chemistry.
breedingNotes: The Long-Band Rasbora is an egg-scattering spawner with no parental care, and breeding in captivity is considered difficult. A separate, dimly lit breeding tank with fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or hornwort, or spawning mops, is recommended. Conditioning prospective pairs with live and frozen foods in the weeks before spawning improves success. Water should be very soft and slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 1–4, temperature 24–26°C). Adults will readily consume their own eggs, so parents should be removed immediately after spawning is complete. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours, and fry become free-swimming within a further 24–48 hours. Start fry on infusoria or commercial fry food, progressing to micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Females are typically deeper-bodied than males, which is the main visual difference between the sexes.

primary.name: FishBase — Rasbora einthovenii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided maximum size (10.6 cm SL), temperature range (22–26°C), pH (6.0–6.5), hardness (dH 0–5), native range (Malay Peninsula and Indonesia), habitat (forest streams), diet (worms, crustaceans, insects), taxonomy (Danionidae/Rasborinae, Cypriniformes). Family retained as Cyprinidae per project convention. Adult aquarium size set at 6–8 cm based on corroborating hobbyist sources.

additional.name: Marine Life ID — Brilliant Rasbora (Rasbora einthovenii)
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Southeast Asia, Malaysia, Indonesia), slow-moving to stagnant vegetated waters, peaceful schooling temperament (groups of 6–10+), planted tank requirement. Used as corroborating source for habitat and social behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: The Long-Band Rasbora scatters eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops and provides no parental care. A dedicated breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5) is recommended, and adults should be removed promptly after spawning to prevent them from eating the eggs. Eggs hatch within 24–48 hours, and fry should initially be fed infusoria or a commercial fry food. Conditioning breeders with live foods in the period leading up to spawning is generally advised to improve spawn success.
